item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: NUKE-new.PNG
Author(s): United States Army, Roget
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Roget as a cropped and grayscaled version of the image below

Source: United States Army
License: Public Domain
Title: N/A
Author: United States Army

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I like it. Unless there are serious objections, that is slated in addition to the Coldest War link.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Adding on, I noted similarities between this and SCP-1984. Both are Soviet technology centred on nuking the Western powers. 1178 might have been a prototype of 1984, which somehow operated differently from what was expected of it.

To apply that as practical suggestion, I propose this:

The early nuclear detection systems of any former Soviet state will begin to indicate an imminent nuclear strike.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Call me crazy, but it seems to me that someone who enjoys an article that heavily features the Cold War, may also enjoy the content of a canon built around the events of the Cold War. Therefore, I don't think its unreasonable to sneak a "Coldest War" inline link into this article. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Prior to SCP-1178's containment, several incidents of near-accidental nuclear strikes by world powers were reported to the Foundation by assets integrated in the Soviet GRU and the American CIA, leading to the discovery and recognition of SCP-1178 as an anomaly. A partial list of incidents caused by SCP-1178 has been included in this report.

To this:

Prior to SCP-1178's containment, several incidents of near-accidental nuclear strikes by world powers were reported to the Foundation by assets integrated in the Soviet GRU and the American CIA, leading to the discovery and recognition of SCP-1178 as an anomaly. A partial list of incidents caused by SCP-1178 has been included in this report.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: something something captive nations

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the imperialists' fault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the USSR's fault

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hang on a moment while I pick these pieces of my brain off the floor and wall. I wonder how those got there…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's trying to determine whether it's ever was a missile

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn you, sir.

item.title: what
item.content:encoded: what

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A missile was involved

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Care to elaborate, or is this the part where you quit answering questions and leave me intrigued but confused forever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really a missile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some missiles aren't looking for anything logical, like targets. They can't be shot down, deactivated, reasoned or negotiated with. Some missiles just want to watch the world burn.

It had to be said. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So is this a Zeno's Arrow Missile or something?
For those who do not know of Zeno's Arrow… it is kind of confusing… and since I only get the concept and not the full issue.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zeno%27s_paradoxes
Arrow paradox

If everything when it occupies an equal space is at rest, and if that which is in locomotion is always occupying such a space at any moment, the flying arrow is therefore motionless.

– as recounted by Aristotle, Physics VI:9, 239b5

In the arrow paradox (also known as the fletcher's paradox), Zeno states that for motion to occur, an object must change the position which it occupies. He gives an example of an arrow in flight. He states that in any one (durationless) instant of time, the arrow is neither moving to where it is, nor to where it is not.[13] It cannot move to where it is not, because no time elapses for it to move there; it cannot move to where it is, because it is already there. In other words, at every instant of time there is no motion occurring. If everything is motionless at every instant, and time is entirely composed of instants, then motion is impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I like anything related to the Soviets, but I can't do anything but downvote this. It's too close to 1984, which IMO was done much better. Also, this one feels threatening enough that even the Foundation would rather destroy it than merely contain it. Yes I know the motto isn't "Destroy Destroy Destroy" but even the Foundation destroys some things! If 682 is deemed dangerous enough to warrant destruction when it's just one lizard, then surely a hovering missile that's always on the verge of causing a nuclear holocaust is worth just shooting down.

Downvoted AND removed from headcanon. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially downvoted this one for being kind of stupid despite it being good, and then saw the comments here saying it was too similar to SCP-1984. So I read 1984, which is more highly rated, and that one was even more stupid than this one. So I upvoted this one because at least it's believable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you have been thinking of SCP-1417-j?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of the…thing that starts doing things unless it feels like it's being studied scientifically, resulting in cheesy movie emulation to keep it contained. I can't remember what the object was, or if it was mainlist or a -J, or what it does if it doesn't feel satisfied, but I do remember the article was a lot more interesting than this one (I've read hundreds of these things, okay? A scip's memorable if it comes to my mind at all). I also seem to recall that the aforementioned object wasn't created to do anything close to the things it does, to anyone's knowledge…Wasn't it like a meteor or something?

This, on the other hand, is a missile that randomly got too attached to its job. It's certainly weird, it passes the SCP litmus test (barely), but…it's not interesting. What also struck me at the start was the degree of "OMG this thing could destroy the world!!!" vibes this article gave off - my very first thought while reading the containment procedures was "This is trying way too hard to be scary/dangerous."

If the missile hadn't been created by man, or actually seemed to have a real intention in mind, or we could communicate with it, or something else suggesting sapience of some sort, then…it would be cliché. Huh. You know, I don't think there's any right way to do this scip…I'm getting wiser and more SCP-savvy with time, so rookie-beginner-noob-idiot moves aren't quite as common and mostly not as severe for me as they once were, but even if this may be such a move, downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The do-not-enter area is indicated in various places to be a circle (sphere? column?) with a radius of ~8km (200 square km), 20 km radius and 200 km radius. Pls to feex?

Having said that, I'm a sucker for anything that's Soviet-era and not awful and this is Soviet-era and nifty, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. It's a Keter whose danger to the world is imminent and apparent. Upvoted

item.title: End of the world
item.content:encoded: You do make reference to the term 'XK-class event' to refer to the end of the world, but you also refer to such an eventuality by other epithets. For clarity and formatting, I think it would be best to refer to what the SCP is capable of indirectly causing as "an XK-class end-of-world scenario" rather than the less formal "apocalyptic scenario" or "the end of the world". I've only ever seen such scenarios referred to by that designation, so if I'm out of line, please let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one quite a lot, especially how the containment is to convince it that it's already caused a nuclear war.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that it's preeeeety close conceptually to 1984, but I still liked it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shoot it down already

Secure contain protect, not destroy destroy destroy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad I'm not the only one who noticed that.

This all seems like a huge amount of effort to contain something that doesn't even have a direct effect. It's just floating there. Shoot it down already. Sorry, I just don't buy the threat of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This problem really bothers me, and that plus the similarity to 1984 earns the downvote.

I'm not sure how you could make this more distinct, but it's not "its own article" enough, if that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i feex

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pic's broken

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it would better, in my opinion, if the rocket was flying in circles, never running out of fuel.

Also instead of the Pretia Protocol sabotaging early warning systems (surely an early warning in itself!) perhaps just have SCP Foundation controlling the early warning systems (either directly or indirectly). After-all they already control a former soviet missile base.

The SCP's primary effect also seems redundant. At no point does the rocket's movement have any consequence other than triggering the secondary effect.

The use of the word acceleration threw me a lot. If an object stops accelerating it doesn't mean it stops moving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A 200 square kilometer area of exclusion has been created around SCP-1178.

Aircraft patrolling the area must never move closer than 200 kilometers to SCP-1178.

These are very different. One of these is a large but reasonable exclusion zone. The other is the size of Saudi Arabia.

For the article itself, I find it pretty generic. Sabotaging the entire former Soviet Union's missile defense system seems iffy, and I'm still unclear on the motivation for making this thing. Screwing up your own side's systems seems sort of counterproductive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the implications behind this, especially in regards to the containment procedures.

I think the article could use some tonal cleanup, and the part explaining the Pretia Protocol should maybe probably be up in the containment. Other than that, I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was well done, but i agree that it is rather close to 1984, and i enjoyed 1984 more due to it's secondary 1984-1 manifestation, so i have no reason to downvote your scp, but i also can't upvote it in good conscience when i prefer a very similar scp.

Well done, and if you can find a way to differentiate it further from 1984, you'll have my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This one's interesting…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's just different enough from SCP-1984 to work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, crap. I knew this reminded me of something, but I thought it was a SCP that got deleted.

Hmm. Removing my upvote for now, but not downvoting. It's well done, but really too close to 1984.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It, too, reminded me of http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-1984. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, but we already have SCP-1984.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. I'm not normally the kind of person to just say "meh", but I really don't have much else to say here.

item.title: Re: To clarify...
item.content:encoded: Sounds like a plan.

item.title: Re: To clarify...
item.content:encoded:
No-one looking at that picture (not even a military expert) would be able to tell what sort of missile it is. It's too small and low quality for that. However, they'll probably note (like WR and I did) that the missile identified on the page is an SLBM, and one that wasn't even around in 1967.

Therefore, I'd advise changing it to something period- and role-appropriate; for example, the SS-13 Savage (RT-2) or SS-7 Saddler (R-16).

item.title: Re: To clarify...
item.content:encoded: The picture is of a bulava, and it was the best picture I had. I don't speak russian, so forgive me if I miss the point of your post.

item.title: To clarify...
item.content:encoded: Are you sure you wanted the sub-launched Булава (RSM-56 Bulava) instead of the land-launched РТ-2УТТХ Тополь-М (RT-2UTTKh Topol-M)? I only ask because the Булава was developed for the Борей-class nuclear submarine, and those aren't deployed yet. (They're thinking of switching them over to the Синева until the Лайнер is ready, due to issues with the Булава.) Plus, the РТ-2УТТХ Тополь-М is HUGE!
